intent:
The harshest form of the user's repro, and the variant most likely to
reproduce "movie will not play".

`zoomed_out_thumb_storm.toml` waits 25 s after zooming out before opening
quickview — which turns out to be long enough for the tier-1 flood to
drain substantially, so that run measured the storm's *tail*, not its
peak. This scenario removes the settle entirely: zoom out four times and
press space on the very next step, while all ~136 tier-1 thumb requests
(the atlas budget bound) are freshly queued and all 3 workers are inside
`made` ffmpeg jobs on the SMB volume.

That is the moment the user actually describes: the screen is still full
of 'loading' tiles when they click. Everything that could starve the open
is at maximum — the worker pool is committed to multi-second seeked
extracts, the SMB link is saturated by 3 concurrent ffmpeg readers, and
the live lane's `SeekablePlayer` must now win reads on that same link
while the render thread does its `clip_meta` stat.

Read, in order:
  - `selected_served` — vs the storm run's 200 ms and `cold_open_quickview`'s
    uncontended number. This is "will not play", quantified.
  - `decode reads` >100ms / >1s and `max` — the video thread blocking in
    `av_read_frame`. The storm run already showed one 1.01 s read at the
    storm's tail; this is the same measurement at its peak.
  - `tick_ms max` + `render_stall_max_us` — the UI thread half. If a frame
    equals a render stall to within a millisecond, that frame WAS one
    synchronous fs call (review 04 §2's signature).
  - `queue wait: thumb` max — starvation of the work the user is watching.

`validity.require` deliberately omits `selected_served`: if the movie never
plays, that is the finding, and the run must still be valid enough to read.
